spec_match.rationale: Fetches data URLs and reads `response.headers.get('content-type')` to verify the full MIME type with parameters comes through — directly testing the Fetch-spec behaviour the feature implements.

interactive.rationale: Six preset buttons, a custom URL input with fetch-and-inspect / clear actions, and a live result table that accumulates rows — a real explorer.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises inspecting the content-type the browser assigns plus comparing to older Chrome — delivered via the per-row Parameters-preserved? column.

edge_cases.rationale: Six presets cover text/html/csv/json/svg with charset, but the preservation check uses a naive `ct.includes(v)` that will false-positive when a parameter value happens to be a substring of the MIME type (e.g. checking for `utf-8` against `application/utf-8-something`). No edge case for: percent-encoded parameter values, multi-parameter URLs, parameter names with case differences.

references.rationale: Links to https://chromestatus.com/feature/4874471565557760 and the Fetch spec data-URL section.

design_system.rationale: Variables-driven, preset buttons rely on the global button rule, no static-card pattern.

openQuestions.title: Tighten the parameter-preservation check
openQuestions.detail: `params.every(p => ct.includes(p.split('=')[1] || p))` will incorrectly report 'preserved' if a parameter value is a substring of the MIME type or another parameter. Re-parse the content-type properly (key=value pairs) and compare each parameter explicitly so the verdict matches reality on adversarial inputs.

openQuestions.title: Add a multi-parameter preset
openQuestions.detail: All current presets have a single parameter. Add `multipart/mixed; boundary=abc; charset=utf-8` and `application/calendar+xml; profile=urn:...; charset=utf-8` so the explorer demonstrates parameter ordering and multi-value preservation.

openQuestions.title: Show the percent-encoded parameter case
openQuestions.detail: A parameter value containing `;` or `,` should be percent-encoded; what happens when authors do it wrong? Add a 'malformed URL' preset that exercises the lenient parser path so visitors learn the boundary.

summary: Solid working explorer with real fetched content-type readouts. Two improvements: the preservation check needs to parse content-type properly (not substring-include) and the preset list should include multi-parameter URLs to show ordering.
